questions.text: What are the main points of this video? What does MKBHD mean by 'peak slab phone' and what's his overall argument?
questions.why_verifiable: Answerable against the transcript's core thesis: the five pillars of a great smartphone (display, battery, build, performance, cameras), that four are 'box checked', and that the camera watermark of matching a real camera can never be fully achieved due to physics.

questions.text: What are the specs of the Oppo Find X9 Ultra in this video, especially the battery size and the camera setup on the back?
questions.why_verifiable: Transcript states concrete numbers: 7,050mAh battery, Snapdragon 8 Elite Gen 5, 6.8-inch OLED, and five rear sensors (200MP main, 200MP 3x telephoto, 50MP 10x with sensor-shift stabilization, 50MP ultrawide, plus a dedicated true-color sensor), and ~100W wired / 50W wireless charging.

questions.text: Does he think phone cameras can actually replace a real professional camera? I heard he compares this phone to a Hasselblad he owns.
questions.why_verifiable: Transcript has a concrete named claim: Oppo licenses Hasselblad and bases the phone on the Hasselblad X2D Mark II, which MKBHD owns; he calls them 'opposite cameras' (the X2D has no computational photography and no video mode) and says a smartphone will never replace a high-end pro camera because of physics, with the iPad-vs-laptop and Stuff Made Here basketball hoop analogies.

answers.answer:
OVERVIEW: This is MKBHD's review-essay built around the Oppo Find X9 Ultra, which he calls "peak slab phone, for now anyway" [00:05] after testing it for almost a month [00:11]. Segments, in order:

1) INTRO / THE PHONE [00:05-00:29]: The Find X9 Ultra is Oppo's newest maxed-out flagship that "makes Samsung's S26 Ultra feel not so ultra" [00:14]: Snapdragon 8 Elite Gen 5, 7,000 mAh battery (rounded; later given precisely as 7,050 mAh [04:04]), 6.8-in super bright OLED, and a 200-megapixel camera system [00:19-00:26]. On-screen at [00:13] the back shows the OPPO and HASSELBLAD branding around a huge circular camera module.

2) WHAT "PEAK SLAB PHONE" MEANS [00:29-01:17]: A "slab phone" is the conventional non-folding phone, and "peak" is a moving goalpost [00:31-00:33]. He recently called the Oppo Find N6 "peak foldable" because its downsides (bezels, cameras, crease) were engineered away [00:33-00:51]. "Peak slab" is harder to define since a better slab always comes next year [00:57-01:00]; his claim: we've hit basically every watermark of a great smartphone except one, and ("hot take") we will never hit that last one [01:09-01:17].

3) TRENDS THAT CAME/WENT VS STUCK [01:19-01:39]: Curved screens and pop-up cameras came and went; ultra-wide cameras stuck [01:25-01:32] (on-screen: a pop-up-camera phone at [01:29] and an iPhone 11 dual camera at [01:31]). We've settled on a "final form" of the maxed-out flagship [01:34-01:39].

4) THE FIVE PILLARS FRAMEWORK [01:44-01:57]: great display, great battery, great build, great performance, great cameras. On-screen content: a blue checklist graphic at [04:38] listing "GREAT DISPLAY (checked), GREAT BATTERY, GREAT BUILD, GREAT PERFORMANCE, GREAT CAMERAS."

5) DISPLAY: box checked [02:10-03:06]. Settled on large flat bright OLED, high refresh/resolution, under-display fingerprint, hole-punch selfie camera [02:32-02:41]; insanely bright outside, down to 1 nit with high-frequency PWM dimming in the dark [02:43-02:50]; occasional extras like privacy displays [02:56]. "Box checked. Watermark achieved." [03:06].

6) BATTERY: box checked [03:08-04:38]. From his HTC Thunderbolt not lasting a school day [03:21] through the 3,000/3,500 mAh era [03:30-03:34], screen-on time going 2→3→4→5 hours [03:37-03:44], then silicon-carbon batteries in the last ~2 years jumping flagships from 5,000 to 6,000+ mAh [03:53-04:01]; the X9 Ultra has 7,050 mAh [04:04]. His battery award went to a 7-8 hr screen-on-time phone that's effectively a 2-day phone [04:14-04:21], plus ~100W wired / 50W wireless charging with the right accessory [04:28-04:31]. On-screen at [04:20]: the phone's battery settings screen at 100%. "Box checked. Watermark reached." [04:38].

7) PERFORMANCE: box checked [04:40-05:06]. Somewhat subjective (software preferences), but high-end chip + smooth animations + responsive display + long updates satisfies most people; year-over-year gains only show in intensive games/benchmarks [04:45-05:06].

8) BUILD: effectively checked [05:06-05:22]. Metal rails, square, built like a tank, slightly improved glass yearly, IP69 dust/water resistance [05:08-05:18] (on-screen at [05:20]: a water-jet IP-test chamber).

9) CAMERAS, THE STUCK PILLAR [05:22-06:44]: Phone launches are now ~20 slides of camera stuff [05:35-05:39]. His shorts experiment: shooting the same picture on every generation of iPhone, Samsung, and Google phone (daylight/lowlight/backlit, 17 phones) [05:48-06:09] (on-screen: year-by-year phone-generation comparison at [05:49] and a grid of labeled iPhones at [06:47]); cameras grew from pinhole webcams to plateau multi-camera systems [06:15-06:23]. The 2026 camera watermark people want: "a smartphone camera system is as good as a regular camera" [06:25-06:31], but "this is a battle against physics that can never fully be won" [06:38-06:44].

10) X9 ULTRA CAMERA DEEP DIVE [06:46-08:26]: Most ridiculous camera system he's seen [06:48]; whole phone built around it (faux leather, orange accent, sideways Hasselblad/Oppo text) [06:56-07:02]; five sensors, each best or among best on paper [07:05-07:39] (full spec list in Q2). X9 Pro won his smartphone camera of the year; this is its Ultra version with the same tasteful minimal processing plus better hardware [07:45-07:55]. Caveats: 2-3 s processing wait, occasionally HDR-y [08:02-08:06]. Key distinction: "super versatile" and "as good quality as regular camera" are two different things [08:19-08:26].

11) THE HASSELBLAD IRONY [08:28-09:43]: Oppo licenses Hasselblad branding and models the phone on the Hasselblad X2D Mark II, which MKBHD actually owns; he says they're "opposite cameras" and a smartphone "will never actually replace a high-end professional camera" (details in Q3). On-screen at [11:09]: an OPPO press release headline "OPPO and Hasselblad Extend Strategic Partnership, Co-developing the Next-Generation Imaging System" (dated Jul 22, 2025).

12) THE UPDATED WATERMARK [09:45-10:36]: A truly great smartphone camera "minimizes the misses and maximizes the makes" [09:51-09:54], like Stuff Made Here's self-moving basketball hoop where you can't miss [09:56-10:07] (on-screen: the hoop footage with a STUFFMADEHERE watermark and "NOT SPED UP" caption at [10:07]). Enthusiasts dislike the over-processed look [10:18-10:22]; the redefined watermark: "a great smartphone camera can take a really good photo or video anytime" [10:30-10:36].

13) LENS ATTACHMENTS [10:40-11:39]: Phones defy physics with portrait mode and Hasselblad lens-emulation bokeh modes [10:44-10:51], plus literal lens attachment accessories: Oppo with Hasselblad, Vivo with Zeiss [10:55-11:09]. The X9 Ultra has a specialized case, lens attachment, and battery grip with a two-stage shutter button [11:11-11:16], enabling 400 mm zoom photos "technically shot on your smartphone" [11:22-11:28]. Note a small conflict: he says "Vivo X300 Pro" [11:00], but the on-screen sample-photo watermark at [11:24] reads "vivo X300 Ultra | ZEISS"; the on-screen watermark supports X300 Ultra, so the spoken "Pro" is likely a misstatement. Bottom line: it's still "mimicking the real thing"; the strength is versatility [11:33-11:39].

14) THE iPAD-AS-LAPTOP PARALLEL [11:42-12:34]: Turning an iPad into a laptop "kind of" works (multi-window, near-desktop browser, file manager) but nobody sane calls it a one-to-one replacement for a high-end pro laptop [11:50-12:17]; yet for regular tasks it's as good as anyone needs [12:19-12:34]. Same for the smartphone camera [12:36].

15) CONCLUSION [12:37-13:11]: Professional cameras will always have their place (he shoots these videos on them) [12:37-12:42], but he shoots his Auto Focus channel exclusively on smartphones, and the latest episode was shot on this X9 Ultra [12:44-12:56]. He invites comments on "peak slab phone in 2026" [13:05-13:06].

OVERALL ARGUMENT: "Peak slab phone" = the settled final form of the conventional flagship where all the pillar watermarks are achieved. His thesis: display, battery, performance, and build are all "box checked," but the last pillar's popular watermark, a phone camera as good as a real camera, is physically unachievable [01:09-01:17, 06:38-06:44]. So the goal should be redefined: not replacing real cameras, but reliably taking a really good photo or video anytime [10:30-10:36]. By that standard, this phone is about as "peak slab" as it gets, for now [00:05].

Specs of the Oppo Find X9 Ultra stated in the video:

CORE SPECS [00:17-00:26]: Snapdragon 8 Elite Gen 5 chip, a 6.8-inch super bright OLED display, and a 200-megapixel camera system on the back. He'd tested it almost a month by publication [00:11-00:12].

BATTERY: There are two figures in the evidence. In the intro he says "7,000 mA battery" [00:19]; in the dedicated battery segment he gives the precise number: "The X9 Ultra here has a 7,050 mAh battery" [04:04]. These reconcile easily: 7,050 mAh at [04:04] is the exact spec and is the figure the primary evidence supports; the [00:19] "7,000" is a rounded intro mention, not a contradiction. It's a silicon-carbon-era battery, part of the wave that pushed flagships from 5,000 to 6,000+ mAh in a few months [03:53-04:04]. Charging context (stated about phones of this class, with this phone shown in hand): ~100 W wired or 50 W wireless "like this with the right accessory" [04:28-04:31]. On-screen content: at [04:20] the phone displays its battery settings screen fully charged at 100% with power-saving options and a usage graph (UI he never reads aloud).

REAR CAMERA SETUP, five sensors, "each one of them is either the best or among the best numbers on paper I've ever seen" [07:05-07:11]:
1. 200 MP main camera, the biggest 200 MP sensor ever in a phone, with a wide aperture and OIS [07:11-07:18].
2. 200 MP 3X telephoto, "literally the biggest telephoto sensor we've ever seen in a phone, bigger than a lot of phones' main cameras" [07:18-07:27].
3. 50 MP 10X (second zoom) camera with sensor-shift stabilization [07:29-07:31].
4. 50 MP ultrawide [07:33].
5. A dedicated color sensor Oppo calls the "true color camera," for more accurate white balance in photos and videos [07:36-07:40].

DESIGN AROUND THE CAMERA [06:54-07:02]: the whole phone is built around the camera system: faux leather back, orange accent, and the Hasselblad and Oppo text set sideways. On-screen: frames at [00:13] and [07:33] show the back with the sideways "HASSELBLAD" lettering, the huge circular multi-lens module with knurled ring, and the orange accent visible at [07:18]. Hasselblad licensing, matching accent color, and knurling around the camera ring are confirmed at [08:33-08:39].

CAMERA BEHAVIOR/QUALITY NOTES: same tasteful, relatively minimal processing as the X9 Pro (his smartphone camera of the year winner) but with better hardware [07:45-07:55]; processing can take 2-3 seconds to finalize a shot [08:02-08:05]; can get a bit HDR-y sometimes [08:06]; huge versatility across focal lengths [08:16].

ACCESSORY ECOSYSTEM: a specialized case, lens attachment, and battery grip with a two-stage shutter button [11:11-11:16], enabling ~400 mm zoom photos [11:22-11:25]; also Hasselblad modes with lens emulation to mimic the bokeh of bigger lenses [10:48-10:51]. On-screen at [11:15] the full white kit is shown: phone case, telephoto lens attachment, grip, strap, and mounting ring.

Other build/rating details (IP69, etc.) are discussed generically for flagships [05:15-05:18], not explicitly stated for this phone specifically. Pricing, exact display refresh rate/resolution numbers, and aperture values are not in this evidence.

No. His position is explicit: "A smartphone will never, I'll just say it, will never actually replace a high-end professional camera at what it does. It's just physics" [09:26-09:33]. Pro camera sensors get bigger and better every year too [09:35-09:39], and he thinks that's true of "everything probably over like an RX100 type of camera" [09:40-09:43]. Earlier he frames the popular 2026 watermark, "a smartphone camera system is as good as a regular camera" [06:25-06:31], as "a battle against physics that can never fully be won," even though phones are "doing a hell of a job of getting close" [06:38-06:46].

The Hasselblad you heard about is real, but the framing matters: it's Oppo, not him, drawing the comparison. Oppo wants you to think of the X9 Ultra as a Hasselblad camera; they bought the licensing, use the same accent color, and put knurling around the camera ring [08:28-08:39], and Oppo's own website says the phone is based on the Hasselblad X2D Mark II [08:39-08:44]. MKBHD says "I actually owned the Hasselblad X2D Mark II" [08:45-08:48], and his verdict is the opposite of Oppo's marketing: "these are like opposite cameras. They could not be further apart" [08:49-08:53]. His reasoning: a smartphone camera's whole point is to be the most capable, versatile, convenient camera possible, a computer trying its hardest to never let you take a bad picture, i.e., computational photography [08:53-09:12]; the X2D Mark II is a professional, very deliberate camera with no computational photography and not even a video mode [09:15-09:23]. So "it's actually kind of a mistake to compare these two" [09:23-09:26]. On-screen support: at [11:09] an OPPO press release is shown headlined "OPPO and Hasselblad Extend Strategic Partnership, Co-developing the Next-Generation Imaging System" (dated Jul 22, 2025), on-screen text he never reads aloud. (Note: the transcript slightly garbles whether he says "owned" or "own" the X2D Mark II [08:45]; either way, ownership is his stated basis for the comparison.)

What he thinks phone cameras SHOULD chase instead: a great smartphone camera "minimizes the misses and maximizes the makes" [09:49-09:54], like Stuff Made Here's self-moving basketball hoop that makes every shot regardless of skill [09:56-10:07] (on-screen: the hoop footage labeled "NOT SPED UP" with a STUFFMADEHERE watermark at [10:07]); the updated watermark is that it "can take a really good photo or video anytime" [10:30-10:36]. Even the fun physics-defying tricks (portrait mode simulating a larger sensor, Hasselblad lens-emulation bokeh modes, and the literal lens attachments/battery grip enabling 400 mm shots) are "still mimicking the real thing"; the strength remains versatility [10:44-11:39]. He compares it to turning an iPad into a laptop: you can kind of do it, and it's as good as most people need, but nobody sane calls it a one-to-one replacement for a pro laptop [11:44-12:17, 12:19-12:34].

His own practice matches the stance: professional cameras "will always have their place. That's what I keep shooting these videos with" [12:37-12:42], while he shoots his Auto Focus channel exclusively on smartphones for fun and to show how good they keep getting; the latest episode was shot on this X9 Ultra [12:44-12:56].

OVERALL ARGUMENT: MKBHD uses the Oppo Find X9 Ultra as the springboard to argue that the standard 'slab' smartphone has essentially reached its final, fully-optimized form. 'Peak slab phone' [00:02] is his term (a deliberate echo of calling the Oppo Find N6 'peak foldable' a couple weeks earlier [00:34-00:45]) for the moment the default high-end flagship hits every 'watermark' users have been chasing. He stresses it's 'a moving goalpost' [00:32] since a better slab will exist next year [00:58-01:01], but says we've achieved basically all of the watermarks except one, and (his 'hot take') we will never achieve that last one [01:10-01:16]: a phone camera as good as a real camera, because that's 'a battle against physics that can never fully be won' [06:37-06:40]. His resolution: redefine the camera watermark as 'a great smartphone camera can take a really good photo or video anytime' [10:26-10:36], and by that standard we're at peak slab phone.

SEGMENTS, in order:
1. Intro to the Oppo Find X9 Ultra: tested almost a month [00:10]; a maxed-out flagship that 'makes Samsung's S26 Ultra feel not so ultra' [00:14-00:16]; Snapdragon 8 Elite Gen 5, 7,050mAh battery, 6.8-inch OLED, 200MP camera system [00:19-00:23]. On-screen: he handles the phone's huge circular rear camera module [00:10] and an orange faux-leather variant with vertical 'OPPO' branding [00:37].
2. 'Peak slab phone' definition vs peak foldable [00:32-01:16].
3. Trends that came and went (curved screens, pop-up cameras) vs stuck (ultrawide cameras); phones settled on a 'final form' [01:26-01:38]. His framework: the 'five pillars' of a great smartphone: display, battery, build, performance, cameras [01:41-01:56]. On-screen content: a blue checklist graphic listing GREAT DISPLAY / GREAT BATTERY / GREAT BUILD / GREAT PERFORMANCE / GREAT CAMERAS appears with boxes checked as he goes; at [04:46] only GREAT DISPLAY is checked, and by [10:35] all five boxes are shown checked, visually matching his 'box checked, watermark achieved' refrain.
4. Display pillar: everyone wanted a screen that 'looks good all the time' [02:16-02:22]; settled on large flat bright OLED, high refresh rate, under-display fingerprint reader, hole-punch selfie camera, 1-nit low brightness with high-frequency PWM dimming [02:33-02:50]. Box checked [03:06].
5. Battery pillar: from his HTC Thunderbolt dying before end of school day [03:20] through the 3,000/3,500mAh barriers [03:30-03:34], to silicon-carbon batteries in the last two years pushing flagships from 5,000 to 6,000mAh [03:53-04:02], with the X9 Ultra at 7,050mAh [04:04]; 7-8 hours screen-on-time award phone, effectively a two-day phone [04:14-04:19]; 100W wired / 50W wireless charging as backup [04:28-04:30]. Box checked [04:39].
6. Performance pillar: more subjective, but high-end chip + smooth animations + long updates = box checked; gains now only show in intensive games/benchmarks [04:40-05:05].
7. Build pillar: metal rails, square 'built like a tank,' incrementally better glass, IP69 dust/water resistance [05:07-05:19].
8. Cameras pillar, the sticking point: presentations are now ~20 slides of camera [05:35-05:37]; his Shorts experiment photographing with every generation of iPhone, Samsung, and Google phones (17 phones in a row; daylight, low-light, backlit) [05:40-06:09]; cameras ballooned from 'a literal pinhole webcam' to a huge multi-camera plateau [06:16-06:22]. The 2026 watermark people want: 'as good as a regular camera,' which physics won't allow [06:24-06:40].
9. X9 Ultra camera tour: whole phone built around the camera (faux leather, orange accent, sideways Hasselblad/Oppo text) [06:53-07:01]; five sensors, each best-or-near-best on paper [07:05]: 200MP main, 200MP 3x telephoto (biggest telephoto sensor in a phone), 50MP 10x with sensor-shift stabilization, 50MP ultrawide, plus a dedicated 'true color' sensor [07:12-07:38]. On-screen content: a caption card reads '200 MP F/1.5 MAIN CAMERA' [07:15]; the f/1.5 aperture is never spoken aloud (he only says 'wide aperture' [07:17]). The X9 Pro won his smartphone camera of the year; this is its ultra version [07:45-07:49]. Caveats: 2-3 second processing finalization, occasionally 'HDR-y' [08:00-08:05]; versatile is not the same as regular-camera quality [08:20-08:25].
10. Hasselblad comparison: Oppo licenses Hasselblad and bases this on the X2D Mark II [08:39-08:42], which he owns [08:45]; they're 'opposite cameras' [08:50], computational photography vs a deliberate pro camera with no computational photography and no video mode [09:05-09:22]; 'the smartphone will never actually replace a high-end professional camera' [09:28-09:30], though phones probably do win over an RX100-type compact [09:42]. On-screen: the Hasselblad X2D sits on a table next to the phone [08:30] and he handles it [09:20].
11. Redefining the watermark: minimize misses, maximize makes [09:50], illustrated by Stuff Made Here's self-moving basketball hoop [09:54-10:04] (on-screen: the clip with a 'STUFFMADEHERE' watermark [10:10]); enthusiasts dislike the over-processed look [10:17]; new watermark = great photo/video anytime [10:26-10:36].
12. Lens attachments and fun extras: portrait mode and Hasselblad lens-emulation modes [10:44-10:52]; Oppo (Hasselblad) and Vivo (Zeiss) lens-attachment ecosystems [11:00-11:07]; case + lens attachment + battery grip with two-stage shutter [11:11-11:16]; 400mm zoom photos 'technically shot on your smartphone' [11:22-11:27]; still 'mimicking the real thing' [11:34]. On-screen note: he says 'Vivo X300 Pro' [11:00], but the sample photo shown carries a watermark reading 'vivo X300 Ultra | ZEISS 2026.04.09' [11:24]; the on-screen watermark suggests the spoken 'Pro' may be a slight misstatement (or a different device in the b-roll), both moments flagged here.
13. iPad-as-laptop analogy: you can kind of turn an iPad into a laptop, and for most people's regular use it's as good as anyone will need, but nobody sane calls it a one-to-one replacement for a pro laptop; 'and that's a smartphone camera' [11:42-12:35]. On-screen: iPad with keyboard case b-roll [12:39].
14. Outro: pro cameras will always have their place (he shoots these videos on them) [12:37-12:40]; he shoots the Auto Focus channel exclusively on smartphones, latest episode on this X9 Ultra [12:45-12:55]; asks viewers what they think of peak slab phone in 2026 [13:06].

Specs of the Oppo Find X9 Ultra as stated in this video:

CORE SPECS (intro rundown [00:19-00:23]):
- Chip: Snapdragon 8 Elite Gen 5 [00:19]
- Battery: 7,050mAh [00:19], restated as '7,050 milliamp-hour battery' at [04:04], where he contextualizes it against the new silicon-carbon-driven norm of flagships jumping from 5,000 to 6,000mAh [03:53-04:02]
- Display: 6.8-inch 'super bright' OLED [00:22]
- Rear cameras: 'an insane 200-megapixel camera system on the back' [00:23]

REAR CAMERA SETUP, five sensors total, 'each one of them is either the best or among the best numbers on paper' he's seen [07:05-07:10]:
1. 200MP main camera, the biggest 200MP sensor ever in a phone, with a wide aperture and OIS [07:12-07:17]
2. 200MP 3x telephoto, 'literally the biggest telephoto sensor we've ever seen in a phone,' bigger than many phones' main cameras [07:19-07:25]
3. 50MP 10x camera (second zoom) with sensor-shift stabilization [07:27-07:28]
4. 50MP ultrawide [07:32]
5. A dedicated color sensor Oppo calls the 'true color camera,' for more accurate white balance in photos and video [07:34-07:38]

CAMERA-CENTRIC DESIGN: the whole phone is built around the camera system: faux leather back, orange accent, and Hasselblad/Oppo text printed sideways [06:53-07:01]. On-screen content (frames, not spoken): the back shows one very large circular camera plateau housing the multiple lenses, seen in his hands at [00:10] and on a shelf at [06:53], where the dark/brown faux-leather variant with the sideways 'Hasselblad' badge inside the camera ring is visible. It's based on (licensed from) the Hasselblad X2D Mark II per Oppo's website [08:39-08:42].

CAMERA BEHAVIOR NOTES: relatively minimal, tasteful processing inherited from the X9 Pro (his smartphone camera of the year winner) with better hardware [07:45-07:55]; shots take 2-3 seconds to finalize processing and can get 'a bit HDR-y' [08:00-08:05].

ACCESSORIES: a specialized case, lens attachment, and battery grip with a two-stage shutter button [11:11-11:16], enabling ~400mm zoom photos [11:22]; Hasselblad lens-emulation modes in software [10:49].

STATED IN GENERAL-FLAGSHIP CONTEXT (not explicitly pinned to this phone's spec sheet): 100W wired / 50W wireless charging, mentioned as what these flagships 'will have,' with the 50W wireless demonstrated 'like this' on camera [04:28-04:30]; IP69 dust/water resistance and metal rails appear in his general build-quality pillar discussion [05:11-05:16]. Evidence is insufficient to confirm those as this phone's exact official numbers.

No conflicts between moments: the battery is given as 7,050mAh in both mentions [00:19], [04:04].

No. His position is explicit: 'The smartphone will never, I'll just say it, will never actually replace a high-end professional camera at what it does' [09:28-09:30]. The reason is physics: phone sensors get bigger and better every year, but so do dedicated camera sensors [09:34-09:38], so the 'as good as a regular camera' watermark is 'a battle against physics that can never fully be won' [06:37-06:40].

You heard right about the Hasselblad. Oppo licenses Hasselblad branding, uses its accent colors and knurled camera ring, and bases the Find X9 Ultra on the Hasselblad X2D Mark II ('they say it on their website') [08:32-08:42]. He actually owns an X2D Mark II [08:45], and his verdict is that they are 'like opposite cameras... they could not be further apart' [08:50-08:51]: the smartphone is a computer doing computational photography, trying its hardest to never let you take a bad picture, maximally versatile and convenient anywhere [08:53-09:11], while the X2D Mark II is a professional, deliberate camera with no computational photography and not even a video mode [09:15-09:22]. He concludes 'it's actually kind of a mistake to compare these two' [09:24].

Important nuances he adds:
- He does think phones probably win 'over, like, an RX100-type of camera' [09:39-09:42], i.e. high-end compacts, just not high-end professional cameras.
- The phone is 'super versatile,' but versatile and 'as good quality as a regular camera' are two different things [08:20-08:25].
- His proposed goal instead: a great smartphone camera 'can take a really good photo or video anytime' [10:26-10:36], minimizing misses like Stuff Made Here's self-moving basketball hoop [09:50-10:04].
- Even with lens attachments (case, lens, battery grip, 400mm zoom shots) it's 'still mimicking the real thing' [11:11-11:34], analogous to an iPad being 'kind of a laptop' but not a one-to-one replacement for a pro laptop, while still being as good as most people need [12:10-12:35].
- Walking the talk: professional cameras 'will always have their place,' and he keeps shooting his main videos on them [12:37-12:40], but he shoots his Auto Focus channel exclusively on smartphones, and its latest episode was shot on this X9 Ultra [12:45-12:55].

No conflicting statements in the evidence: every relevant moment supports 'never replaces a pro camera, but may beat compacts and is more than good enough for most people.' Note: the frames selected for this question show the phone itself [06:53] but do not include an image of the Hasselblad; the comparison is established in the spoken evidence above.

per_question.rationale: Both are thorough, well-ordered summaries that correctly define 'peak slab phone' (settled final form of the flagship, all pillar watermarks achieved except cameras, which physics prevents [01:10-01:16, 06:37-06:40]) and the redefined watermark [10:26-10:36]. A's coverage is marginally richer in spots (privacy display, charging detail) but contains a factual error: it claims the intro states '7,000 mAh (rounded)' when transcript [00:19] says 7,050mAh, and its anchor for the title line ([00:05]) is looser than B's exact [00:02]. B is fully accurate on every spot-checked quote and timestamp ([00:32] moving goalpost, [03:20] Thunderbolt, [04:04] 7,050mAh, [05:35-05:37] 20 slides, [06:16] pinhole webcam), with only a trivially odd visual claim (iPad b-roll at [12:39]) that is unverifiable and inconsequential. B wins on correctness at essentially equal completeness.

The question targets battery size and rear cameras.
